feelGood.name: The Happiness Beyond the Garden
feelGood.brief: Mara pursues success and self focus, yet feels empty until a small act of kindness leads her to community, connection, and the realization that helping others brings deeper, lasting happiness.
story: Mara had always believed that happiness was something you built carefully, like a private garden. You planted what you liked, watered your own ambitions, trimmed away anything that threatened your peace. At least, that’s what she told herself as she sat alone in her apartment on the twelfth floor, staring at the skyline she had worked so hard to earn, She had the job, the view, the quiet. Everything was exactly as she’d planned, And yet, the silence pressed in on her like something unfinished, It hadn’t always been this way. When Mara first moved to the city, she had filled her days with relentless purpose long hours at work, networking events, gym sessions, carefully scheduled downtime. Every action was an investment in herself. Her colleagues admired her discipline. Her parents called her focused. Her social media painted a life that looked, from the outside, like success distilled into neat squares, But the more she achieved, the less it seemed to matter, It was on a rainy Tuesday evening, when the city lights blurred into streaks against her window, that something small shifted, She had just left the office, umbrella forgotten, heels clicking unevenly against the wet pavement. At the subway entrance, she noticed an older man struggling with a cart of books. They were stacked haphazardly, threatening to spill with every bump, Mara slowed—just for a second, She could keep walking. She was tired. She had earned her rest, But something in the man’s quiet frustration caught her, Do you need help? she asked, almost before she could stop herself, He looked up, surprised, as if the offer itself were unusual. If you don’t mind, he said, She didn’t mind, though she had expected to. Together, they maneuvered the cart down the stairs, step by careful step. It took longer than she liked. Her clothes dampened further, her phone buzzed with missed notifications. But when they reached the platform, the man smiled in a way that felt unexpectedly genuine, Thank you, he said. These are for the community center. We’re trying to rebuild the library, Mara nodded, unsure what to say. That’s… nice, It’s more than nice, he replied gently. Books give people a place to go when they can’t go anywhere else, She offered a polite smile and boarded her train. The interaction should have ended there—a small, forgettable detour in her otherwise efficient day, But it didn’t, That night, as she sat with her carefully prepared dinner, she found herself thinking about the cart of books. About the man’s gratitude. About the quiet sense of something she couldn’t quite name, It wasn’t pride. It wasn’t accomplishment, It felt lighter than that, The next day, she returned to her routine. Meetings, deadlines, emails. But something felt slightly off, like a song played just out of tune. By the end of the week, she made a decision that surprised even her, She went looking for the community center, It wasn’t hard to find. Tucked between a laundromat and a closed storefront, it looked modest, almost overlooked. Inside, though, it buzzed with life—children reading, volunteers sorting donations, someone painting a mural along the far wall, And there, near a stack of newly organized shelves, was the man from the subway, He recognized her immediately. You came back, he said, as if it mattered, I had some time, Mara replied, though that wasn’t entirely true, He handed her a small stack of books. Then you can help, It started simply. An hour here, an afternoon there. Shelving books. Helping kids with homework. Carrying boxes. Tasks that, on paper, held no value to her carefully structured life, But something strange began to happen, The silence in her apartment didn’t feel as heavy anymore, Her workdays, once endless, seemed to pass more quickly. She found herself less irritated by small inconveniences, less consumed by her own progress. There was a steadiness growing inside her, a sense that her days were connected to something beyond her own advancement, One evening, a young girl named Lila approached her with a book clutched tightly in her hands, I finished it, she said proudly, That’s great, Mara replied. Did you like it?, Lila nodded. It made me feel brave, Mara blinked, caught off guard. Brave?, Yeah, the girl said simply. Like I could do things I’m scared of, Mara smiled, but the words lingered long after Lila ran off to pick another book, Brave, She had spent years building a life that looked strong from the outside, but she had never considered whether she felt brave inside it. Helping at the center hadn’t been part of her plan. It hadn’t advanced her career or improved her image, And yet, it felt like the most meaningful thing she had done in years, Weeks turned into months. The community center became part of her life, not as an obligation, but as something she looked forward to. She learned names, stories, small details that wove her into the fabric of the place, One night, as she locked up alongside the older man—whose name she had learned was Elias—he glanced at her thoughtfully, You seem different, he said, Different how? Mara asked, Happier, he replied, She considered that. I think I am, Elias nodded, as if this confirmed something he already knew. It’s a strange thing, he said. We spend so much time trying to build happiness for ourselves. But it often shows up when we’re not looking at ourselves at all, Mara laughed softly. I used to think the opposite, Most people do, he said, As she walked home that night, the city felt different. Not quieter, not slower—but fuller. As if, by stepping outside her own carefully maintained world, she had somehow expanded it, When she reached her apartment, she paused by the window, The skyline hadn’t changed. The lights still shimmered in the distance, just as they always had. But the view no longer felt like something she had earned alone. It felt like something she was part of—one piece in a much larger picture, For the first time in a long while, the silence didn’t press in on her, It rested gently around her, like a space she no longer needed to fill, And she realized, with a clarity that surprised her, that the moments she had spent helping others—the inconvenient, unplanned, seemingly small acts—had given her something she had never been able to build on her own, Not success, Not achievement, Something quieter. Stronger, A sense that her life reached beyond herself, And in that, she had found a kind of happiness that no amount of self-focus had ever brought her, The garden she had tried so carefully to cultivate alone had never quite bloomed, But the moment she stepped outside it—into the shared, messy, unpredictable lives of others—something began to grow, Not just for them, But for her, too

feelGood.name: The Power of Yet
feelGood.brief: After failing to make his soccer team, Flint learns through persistence that mistakes are lessons. Embracing failure, he grows stronger, ultimately discovering that resilience and effort lead to real success.
story: Flint had never liked losing, not even a little. To him, losing wasn’t just a moment—it felt like something that stuck to him, something that followed him home and sat quietly in the corners of his thoughts, At twelve years old, he had already decided that winning meant everything. It meant you were good enough. It meant people noticed. It meant you didn’t have to explain yourself, So when Flint tried out for the school soccer team and didn’t make it, it felt like the world had quietly tilted in the wrong direction, The list had been posted outside the gym, names lined up in neat columns. Flint scanned it once, then again, slower this time. His name wasn’t there, At first, he told himself he must have missed it. He checked again, tracing each line carefully. Still nothing, Around him, other kids were celebrating, calling their parents, laughing. Flint stepped back, the noise fading into something distant and hollow, He walked home that day without saying much, his hands shoved deep into his pockets. When his mom asked how it went, he shrugged, I guess I’m just not good enough, he muttered, She didn’t argue with him. Instead, she simply said, Maybe you’re just not good enough yet, Flint frowned at that word. Yet. It sounded like something unfinished, something that refused to settle into a final answer, The next few days, Flint avoided anything that reminded him of soccer. He didn’t watch games, didn’t practice, didn’t even look at the ball sitting in the corner of his room. If he couldn’t win, what was the point, But the feeling didn’t go away, It lingered, not just as disappointment, but as something heavier. A question, maybe. Or a challenge he didn’t want to face, One afternoon, while walking home from school, Flint noticed an older boy at the park juggling a soccer ball with effortless rhythm. The ball moved like it belonged to him, rising and falling with precise control, Flint stopped to watch, The boy dropped the ball suddenly and laughed, shaking his head. Guess that’s what happens when you lose focus, he said, noticing Flint standing nearby, Flint hesitated. Do you ever mess up? he asked, The boy grinned. All the time, Flint frowned. But you’re really good, The boy shrugged. I wasn’t always, Something about that answer stuck with Flint. It didn’t feel like a dismissal. It felt honest, That evening, Flint picked up the soccer ball in his room. It felt strange in his hands, like something he had abandoned too quickly, He stepped outside and tried to juggle it like the boy at the park, He failed immediately, The ball dropped after one awkward bounce, rolling away across the grass. Flint sighed, frustration bubbling up, But then he remembered the boy’s words, All the time, Flint tried again, And again, And again, Each attempt ended the same way at first. The ball slipping, bouncing away, refusing to cooperate. But something was different now. Instead of stopping, Flint kept going, He started noticing things. How the ball felt against his foot. How small adjustments made a difference. How balance mattered more than force, The failures didn’t disappear, but they began to change, They became information, Days turned into weeks. Flint practiced whenever he could—after school, before dinner, sometimes even in the early morning when the world was quiet, He still failed often. The ball still fell. His legs still got tired. But each time, he understood a little more, One afternoon, he managed three juggles in a row, It felt like a victory far bigger than it should have been, He smiled, By the time the next soccer tryouts came around, Flint wasn’t the same boy who had stood silently in front of the list, He was stronger, more confident, but more importantly, he was no longer afraid of failing, When his turn came, he stepped onto the field with steady determination. He made mistakes—missed passes, imperfect kicks—but he didn’t freeze, didn’t give up, He adjusted, He kept going, And for the first time, he understood that winning wasn’t about never failing. It was about what you did after, When the results were posted, Flint walked up to the list again. His heart beat faster, but it wasn’t the same kind of fear, This time, his name was there, Flint exhaled, a quiet laugh escaping him, But the real change wasn’t the list, It was him, Later that evening, Flint returned to the park. The older boy was there again, practicing, Hey, Flint called out, juggling the ball three times before catching it, The boy nodded, impressed. Not bad, Flint grinned. I failed a lot to get that, The boy laughed. That’s how it works, Flint looked down at the ball in his hands, then back up at the open field, For the first time, he didn’t see failure as something to avoid, He saw it as something that pointed the way forward, And as he stepped back onto the grass, ready to try again, he realized something simple but powerful, Every time he had fallen short, he had also taken a step closer, Not just to winning, But to becoming someone who understood how to get there

horror.name: The Dog That Wasn’t There
horror.brief: A tortured boy finds comfort in a loyal dog who protects him from his abusive mother until a chilling revelation shows the savior was imagined, and the boy committed the unthinkable himself.
story: The house always smelled faintly of bleach and something older, something that clung to the walls no matter how often it was scrubbed. Caleb had learned not to ask about it. Questions only made things worse, and worse was something he had come to understand in quiet, careful ways, His mother liked things clean. That’s what she said every time her voice sharpened, every time her hands gripped too tightly, every time discipline stretched beyond anything that felt like discipline at all. Clean house, clean mind, clean child. Caleb tried his best to be all three, but it was never enough, The dog had come one winter evening, thin and shaking, ribs visible beneath patchy fur. Caleb had found him curled near the back steps, barely alive. He had begged to keep him, whispering promises of responsibility, of obedience, of being better. His mother had stared at the animal for a long time before agreeing, though her smile had not reached her eyes, They named him Rusty, though his fur was more dull brown than red. Caleb loved him immediately. Rusty followed him everywhere, silent and watchful, as if he understood things Caleb could not say aloud. When nights grew long and heavy, when the house filled with quiet tension, Rusty would press close, grounding him, But Rusty was not safe either, His mother’s rules extended to everything under her roof. A misplaced shoe, a drop of water on the floor, a sound at the wrong time—anything could trigger her anger. Rusty learned quickly, just as Caleb had, but even perfect stillness did not always protect him. Caleb would watch, frozen, as his mother’s frustration spilled over onto the dog, sharp and sudden, It was then that Rusty began to change, At first, it was small things. A low growl when she raised her voice. A stiffening posture when she entered the room. Caleb noticed, even if he pretended not to. There was something in Rusty’s eyes now, something that hadn’t been there before. Not fear. Not entirely, One night, after a particularly bad evening, Caleb lay on the floor beside Rusty, his body aching, his thoughts drifting somewhere far away. The house was quiet again, but it was the kind of quiet that came after a storm, heavy and waiting, Rusty lifted his head, He looked toward the hallway, toward the closed bedroom door where Caleb’s mother slept. His ears twitched, his body tense. Caleb followed his gaze, heart beginning to race for reasons he couldn’t explain, It’s okay, Caleb whispered, though he wasn’t sure who he was trying to comfort, Rusty did not relax, Days passed, and the tension grew. Caleb felt it in the way Rusty stayed closer now, never wandering far. In the way he watched the mother with an intensity that made Caleb uneasy. It was as if something was building, something inevitable, Then came the night everything changed, The argument had started over something small. It always did. A glass left in the sink. A tone of voice. Caleb couldn’t even remember anymore. What he remembered was the shouting, the sharp crack of something breaking, the sudden, suffocating fear, His mother’s hand came down harder than before. Again and again, until the room blurred. Caleb fell, the world tilting, his breath shallow, And then Rusty moved, It happened so fast Caleb could barely follow it. A blur of fur and sound, a deep, guttural snarl that seemed to fill the entire room. Rusty lunged, placing himself between Caleb and his mother, teeth bared, eyes wild, For a moment, everything froze, Then chaos, His mother screamed, a sound unlike anything Caleb had ever heard. Rusty didn’t stop. He drove forward with a force that felt almost unnatural, fueled by something deeper than instinct. The room became a storm of movement, of noise, of something breaking apart, Caleb watched, unable to move, unable to understand, Rusty was everywhere. His growls, his snapping jaws, the way he refused to back down. His mother stumbled, fell, tried to fight back, but it was too late. Rusty had already decided, And he would not stop, The sounds changed after that. The shouting turned to something else. Something wet, something final. Caleb squeezed his eyes shut, but it didn’t block it out. Nothing could, Time stretched, warped, became meaningless, And then, silence, When Caleb finally opened his eyes, the room was still. The air felt thick, heavy, like it had settled into something permanent. Rusty stood in the center of it all, chest heaving, his body trembling slightly, He turned to Caleb, For a moment, everything softened. The wildness faded, replaced by something gentle, something familiar. Rusty stepped closer, lowering himself beside Caleb, pressing against him the way he always had, It’s over now, Caleb whispered, Rusty didn’t respond. He simply stayed there, steady and warm, until Caleb’s breathing slowed, until the exhaustion pulled him under, Caleb closed his eyes, Sleep came quickly, When the morning light crept through the window, it illuminated a room that no longer resembled what it had been. The quiet was different now. Not heavy, not waiting. Just still, Caleb sat in the center of the floor, motionless, There was no dog beside him, There had never been, The space where Rusty had been lying was empty, untouched, as if nothing had ever occupied it. Caleb’s hands were sticky, darkened with something that had long since dried. The same dark stains covered the floor around him, spreading outward in uneven patterns, His mother lay nearby, unmoving, her form still and final, Caleb blinked slowly, his gaze unfocused. For a moment, it seemed like he might say something, might move, might react to the reality around him, But he didn’t, He simply sat there, surrounded by silence and the remnants of something his mind had needed to create, A protector, A companion, A savior, Rusty had been all of those things, And none of them at all, Caleb’s head tilted slightly, his eyes growing heavy once more. The weight of everything pressed down on him, too much to carry, too much to understand, So he didn’t try, He leaned forward, curling into himself, the cold floor beneath him, the dried blood cracking softly as he shifted. His breathing slowed, steady and quiet, And soon, he was asleep again, Alone in the silence, exactly as he had always been
